patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record's Zstandard compression feature, which caused it to abort recording and produce an undecompressable file due to a 'Bad address' error. The patches also fix two latent bugs: an output buffer overrun and a broken error fallback in the zstd compressor. The changes ensure that multiple compressed records are handled correctly and that raw data is not stored when compression fails.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the perf record tool's ZSTD compression handling. When ZSTD compression fails, it previously attempted to copy the uncompressed data into the output buffer, which can lead to issues such as incorrect header sizes and unbounded copying. Instead, this patch propagates the error by returning -1 when compression fails, preventing these problems.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the zstd compression API used by perf tools, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The issue occurred because the function did not check if there was enough space left in the output buffer to hold a full record before writing one. The patch adds a check to prevent this overwriting and returns an error instead.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are emitted per push when Zstd compression is enabled and the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size. The fix involves changing how the header size is calculated to prevent underflow and truncation, allowing for correct decompression of the data. The patch also updates the test case to reflect this change.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: display NFSv4 callback operation statistics

version_history.subject: [PATCH nfs-utils] nfsstat: display NFSv4 callback operation statistics

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new feature to the nfsstat utility, which displays NFSv4 callback operation statistics. The kernel maintains per-operation call counts for NFSv4 backchannel operations and exports them through the server-stats netlink dump. The patch updates the nfsstat code to report these counts in a 'Server nfs v4 callback operations' section when the stats are fetched via netlink. This feature is only available over netlink, as the /proc interface does not expose equivalent data.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/6] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) call counts for each RPC procedure to the svc_stat structure in the Linux kernel's sunrpc module. The existing global call count arrays are not netns-aware, which is a problem for the upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics. To solve this issue, the patch introduces per-netns equivalents of the global call count arrays and updates relevant code to manage these new counters. This change will enable more fine-grained monitoring and statistics collection in network namespaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/6] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace stat arrays instead of global arrays. This allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts for each network namespace. The change only affects nfsd, which always allocates these arrays, making it safe to use them.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/6] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics through the nfsd generic netlink family. The handler uses a dump operation to stream statistics across multiple netlink messages, allowing for efficient retrieval of server statistics. It also includes support for per-version procedure counts and NFSv4 per-operation counts. This allows the nfsstat tool to retrieve server statistics via netlink with a procfs fallback for older kernels.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/6] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters in various NFS-related modules, as it is no longer accessed by any code. The removal of these fields simplifies the code and reduces memory usage.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/6] nfsd: count NFSv4 callback operations per netns
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-netns counters to track NFSv4 callback operations in the Linux kernel's NFS server implementation. It introduces a new array of percpu counters for callback operations, indexed by RFC 8881 callback opcode, and updates the nfsd4_run_cb() function to bump the relevant counter for each queued callback. The patch also removes redundant statistics tracking for wdeleg_getattr conflicts and drops related code. This change aims to provide more accurate statistics for NFSv4 backchannel operations.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the patch introduces a use-after-free (UAF) possibility, specifically in nfsd4_run_cb(), which increments the per-netns callback counter after freeing the callback structure, and requested addressing this issue before applying the patch.
review_comments.raw_body:
I agree with the Sashiko review that this patch introduces a UAF
possibility that needs to be addressed before the patch is applied.


-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/6] nfsd: export NFSv4 callback op stats via netlink
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for exporting NFSv4 callback operation statistics via netlink, allowing nfsstat to report backchannel operation counts over netlink. The patch introduces a new attribute 'proc4cb-ops' in the server-stats netlink dump, which reuses the existing layout and emits one entry per callback opcode from the per-netns callback counters.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Identified an issue with the loop emitting entries for every opcode, but only those with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance are incremented. Suggested checking what the forechannel counter does for SEQUENCE operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026, at 12:58 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Add a proc4cb-ops nested attribute to the server-stats netlink dump,
> reusing the existing server-proc-entry (op/count) layout. The first dump
> message emits one entry per callback opcode (OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD)
> from the per-netns callback counters; the set is small and fits alongside
> the scalar stats, so no extra paging is needed.
>
> This lets nfsstat report NFSv4 backchannel operation counts over netlink,
> including CB_GETATTR which corresponds to the procfs wdeleg_getattr line.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  Documentation/netlink/specs/nfsd.yaml |  6 ++++++
>  fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c                      | 23 +++++++++++++++++++++++
>  include/uapi/linux/nfsd_netlink.h     |  1 +
>  3 files changed, 30 insertions(+)

> diff --git a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> index 9bedbe7096c3..674fab6a86a5 100644
> --- a/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> +++ b/fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c
> @@ -2476,6 +2476,29 @@ int nfsd_nl_server_stats_get_dumpit(struct sk_buff *skb,
>  			}
>  		}
> 
> +#ifdef CONFIG_NFSD_V4
> +		/* NFSv4 callback (backchannel) per-operation counts */
> +		for (int op = OP_CB_GETATTR; op <= OP_CB_OFFLOAD; op++) {
> +			struct nlattr *nest;
> +			u64 cnt;
> +
> +			cnt = percpu_counter_sum_positive(
> +				&nn->cb_counter[op]);

The loop emits an entry for every opcode in OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD,
but only opcodes with a nfsd4_callback_ops instance get incremented (via
nfsd4_run_cb() keyed on cb->cb_ops->opcode). Opcodes nfsd never sends
correctly report 0. The exception is op 11 / CB_SEQUENCE: it's written by
encode_cb_sequence4args() on every minorversion>0 callback, so it goes
out on the wire but is never counted. The dump always shows op 11 with
count 0, which could be misread as "none sent" rather than "not tracked."

Check what the forechannel counter does for SEQUENCE operations.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink, addressing limitations in the current procfs interface. It adds per-network-namespace procedure call counts to the sunrpc layer and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. The patch also includes tracking of callback operation counts.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Raised a potential use-after-free (UAF) possibility in the patch and suggested addressing it before application.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026, at 12:57 PM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> The NFS server tracks per-operation call counts for the forward channel
> (proc4ops) but keeps no statistics for the NFSv4 backchannel (callback)
> operations it sends to clients.
>
> Add a per-netns array of percpu counters for callback operations, indexed
> by RFC 8881 callback opcode (OP_CB_GETATTR..OP_CB_OFFLOAD), and bump the
> relevant counter in nfsd4_run_cb(), which is hit exactly once per callback
> that is actually queued.
>
> CB_GETATTR is sent when a GETATTR conflicts with an outstanding write
> delegation, which is roughly what the dedicated wdeleg_getattr counter
> tracked. The two are not identical: the old counter incremented on every
> such conflict, whereas the CB_GETATTR counter only counts callbacks that
> are actually queued, so concurrent conflicts that coalesce onto an
> already in-flight CB_GETATTR are now counted once rather than once per
> conflict. Report the procfs "wdeleg_getattr" line from the CB_GETATTR
> counter and drop the now-redundant NFSD_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR counter, its
> helper, and its increment site.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
> ---
>  fs/nfsd/netns.h        | 12 +++++++++++-
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4callback.c |  7 ++++++-
>  fs/nfsd/nfs4state.c    |  2 --
>  fs/nfsd/nfsctl.c       | 14 ++++++++++++++
>  fs/nfsd/stats.c        |  2 +-
>  fs/nfsd/stats.h        |  5 +++--
>  6 files changed, 35 insertions(+), 7 deletions(-)

I agree with the Sashiko review that this patch introduces a UAF
possibility that needs to be addressed before the patch is applied.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/5] kdevops: fixes for new ansible and some nfsd default config changes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] ansible: fix bare conditionals for ansible-core 2.20+ strict booleans
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes issues in Ansible playbooks where bare conditionals were used to evaluate variables that returned strings instead of booleans, causing fatal errors starting from Ansible-core version 2.20. The patch corrects the use of boolean filters and default values to ensure proper evaluation of these variables.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/5] ansible: replace deprecated top-level ansible_* facts with ansible_facts[]
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces deprecated top-level Ansible facts (e.g. ansible_os_family) with the new form using the ansible_facts dictionary, as required by Ansible-core 2.24. The change affects 109 playbook and template files across various roles in the tree.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] gitr: enable verbose logging in the test harness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enables verbose logging in the test harness of the gitr suite, making it easier to diagnose issues when tests fail.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] nfsd: use more reasonable defaults for local VM testing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the default settings for NFS server (nfsd) testing on local VMs to more reasonable values. The number of threads is increased from 8 to 128, and the lease time and grace period are reduced from 90 seconds to 30 seconds. Additionally, version 4.0 of the NFS protocol is enabled by default, and dynamic threading is used with newer kernels. Finally, the mountd service is configured to manage group IDs.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] devconfig: add some extra packages to the kdev tools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds extra packages to the kdev tools installation process, specifically for Red Hat configurations. The added packages include strace, lsof, git, wireshark-cli, rsync, and time, which are useful tools for development and debugging purposes. This change only affects Red Hat distributions as the package names for other distros were unknown at the time of submission.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses issues introduced by new versions of Ansible and NFSd, including a backward-incompatible change in Ansible that requires fixes to conditionals. It also includes optional changes to existing workflows and configuration defaults for local VM testing. The patches remove redundant checks, replace deprecated facts with an array-based approach, enable verbose logging, and update package installations.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: kdevops-ng: graduating kdevops beyond Ansible
Re: kdevops-ng: graduating kdevops beyond Ansible
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch graduates kdevops-ng beyond its current reliance on Ansible, allowing for more flexible and efficient kernel testing. The existing approach using kconfig/makefiles is acknowledged as a 'square peg in a round hole'. The new direction aims to simplify the process by spawning a familiar Linux distribution, making it easier to troubleshoot issues.

review_comments.author: Daniel Gomez
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that Windmill's architecture is complex and not well-positioned in the market, but believes it has useful features for developer workflow orchestration and suggested exploring its potential to turn kdevops into a kernel regression workflow engine.

Reviewer Daniel Gomez expressed concerns about introducing too many new technologies at once (Nix and Windmill) and suggested a way forward: using Windmill as the replacement for Ansible, Makefiles, and Kconfig, while keeping the current toolkit for libvirt/libguestfs/Terraform paths.

Reviewer noted that userland test suites are also important for kernel testing and suggested using Nix to build and pin custom versions of tools, allowing for consistent builds between host and guest.
review_comments.raw_body:
100%.

AFAIK, even Windmill itself struggles a bit to position what it is in
the market. But I think they get some obviously useful things right for
developer workflow orchestration that nobody else does. BTW, before
landing on this project I also looked at others: cijoe [1], Kestra,
and, at a different level, task.dev and just.systems. And these are the
alternatives Windmill compares itself against [2], in case any of them
are familiar to you (none were to me).

For a quick sense of what Windmill is, here's their architecture
diagram: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/misc/architecture

The way I think of it: a GitLab runner plus the YAML pipeline in
.gitlab-ci.yml (or the GitHub Actions equivalent), but for your
development workflows, not your project's CI. Though it can obviously be
used for project's CI too. All in one package, with far more pipeline
("flow", in Windmill terms) features [3]. That last part is one that I'm
also interested in: we could turn kdevops into a kernel regression
workflow engine with git-bisect and generate reports we can submit
directly to the mailing list and maintainers (similar to 0-day). Yes, we
can already do this today with Luis's patches from last year (I tried
them and they work), but it's exactly the kind of workflow where you can
see Ansible isn't the right tool.

Link: https://cijoe.readthedocs.io/en/latest/# [1]
Link: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/compared_to/peers [2]
Link: https://www.windmill.dev/docs/core_concepts#flow-specific-features [3]

---

I understand, and I'm 100% with you: Nix (+ Windmill) is simply too
many new things at once.

The real benefit here is that the guest and the controller run the exact
same environment. You could compare it to virtme-ng with overlayfs,
where, AFAIK, you boot a guest off the host's rootfs (read-only, with
an overlay for writes), so the guest mirrors the host. The difference is
that with virtme-ng that shared environment is still whatever Debian or
Fedora you happen to be running, so it isn't pinned or reproducible;
with Nix it's the same shared environment (same /nix/store).

A way forward could be to use Windmill as the Ansible, Makefile, Kconfig
replacement or the Nix-based paths, and keep the current toolkit for the
libvirt/libguestfs/Terraform paths. Both can coexist.

---

But we also need changes on userland test suites so I think it matters
too. I use Nix for exactly that, to build and pin custom versions
of the test suites and tools (e.g. fstests, fio, bcc, etc.) that I can
run either baremetal or in a VM. The benefit is that the host and guest
share the same /nix/store (the guest mounts it over virtiofs), so a tool
you build on the host is the exact same build the guest runs.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver from io_uring, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, and a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() is introduced to fetch the next request without sending it immediately. This change simplifies the code and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which simplifies the code and centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging. The changes affect several functions in fs/fuse/dev_uring.c, making it easier to maintain and modify the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), making it clearer and more centralized, and also improves error handling and logging.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE file system by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when buffer rings are added. The patch introduces an enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types and modifies existing functions to use this new type. The changes improve code readability and maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function called `setup_fuse_copy_state` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making the code more modular and easier to maintain. The function takes several parameters, including a pointer to the copy state structure, a fuse ring, a request, an entry in the ring, and an iterator. It initializes the copy state and imports user buffers as needed. This patch is part of a larger series of changes to improve the FUSE driver's performance and reliability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The changes are made in the fuse:dev_uring.c file, specifically in the functions fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe and fuse_uring_create_ring_ent. This patch is part of a larger series of 10 patches and has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used as needed by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi noted that the registration interface for FUSE io-uring buffer pools is inconsistent, as it decouples buffers from sqe's but not the registration process itself. He suggested adding a new cmd op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_BUFPOOL_SETUP) to register the buffer pool independently of sqe setup.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> payload in the first sqe.

I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Thanks,
Miklos

FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

> This would have to be returned as part of the struct

> good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the

Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.
So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
"buf_index" for registered buffers?

Miklos

> large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different

Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?

We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

> I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> comes up in the future,

Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
initialization is a good idea regardless.

> different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.

Note, "queue" could mean two things here:

 - fuse's waiting list
 - cqe's on the ring

Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Thanks,

The author proposes adding a 'uint16_t pool_idx' to struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to indicate which pool a buffer belongs to, but notes that this would require userspace to keep track of the starting address of each pool.

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > payload in the first sqe.
>
> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

>

> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > Buffer ring request flow
> > ------------------------
> > |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> > |                                          |
> > |  [client request arrives]                |
> > |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> > |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> > |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> > |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> > |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> > |                                          |  [CQE received]
> > |                                          |  [read headers]
> > |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
>
> How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

>
> Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

>
> We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
> patchset).  What about a new directory:
> Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 6:22 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:38, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
> > allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
> > compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.
>
> But which buffer?   The interface is supposed to handle multiple
> pools, in which case the server needs to find the pool and the buffer
> within the pool.

If we later wanted to add support for multiple buffer pools, we would
need to extend the struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to include a "uint16_t
pool_idx" to indicate which pool. Userspace would be responsible for
keeping track of the starting address of each pool.

>
> FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

For FIXED ops not through zero-copy, there's one index for the entire
pool (eg this is the index where the pool resides). It passes this by
setting the sqe->buf_index.
For FIXED ops through zero-copy, userspace is required to register a
sparse buffer where the kernel should place the application's/page
cache's pages. Userspace determines the index into this sparse buffer
for the sqe and it passes this index to the kernel in the init setup
in the "uint16_t zero_copy_index;" field when it registers the sqe.

Neither require the kernel to pass back a buf index corresponding to
the fixed buffer index. For both cases, userspace knows the index for
the fixed buffer (it is the one who determined it) and it is the one
who passes this info to the kernel at setup time.

>
> > This would have to be returned as part of the struct
> > fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
> > use.
>
> Does the 12 bytes limit come from the cqe?

The 12 byte limit comes from the "struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out"
definition in the uapi [1].

>
> > Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
> > using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
> > buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
> > trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
> > the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
> > good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
> > offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
> > would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.
>
> Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.

For multiple buffer pools, I was thinking that would be through a
separate "uint16_t pool_idx" field to indicate which pool.

> So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
> "buf_index" for registered buffers?

I think we should just pass back "pool index" and have userspace keep
track of the registered buffer id that corresponds to that pool index.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h#L1255

On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 10:07 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Wed, 17 Jun 2026 at 14:50, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> > I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
> > multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
> > [1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
> > allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
> > address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
> > be simpler.
>
> Confused.  I thought this was exactly about multiple bufpools
> (containing different size bufs) per ring.
>
> If not, then what's the point of dynamically assigning bufs to sqe's?

imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
times.

I think we should still make the design for bufpools
forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
comes up in the future, but I think for the majority of
servers/workloads, incremental buffer consumption would meet their
needs and be simpler logic wise and avoids the server having to try to
figure out how to optimally distribute different size bufpools across
differnet queues.

>From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests. The non-IO
queues would only need small buffers (eg <= 4k). Userspace could try
implementing separation with threadpools instead of fuse uring doing
queue-level separation, but I think this adds extra context-switching
overhead and is a lot more complex to get performant. If a server
truly needs multiple buffer sizes beyond just non-IO vs IO separation,
I think having multiple bufpools per queue would be a better choice
than dedicating queues to specific categories of buffer sizes; in the
initial discussion, I had assumed the buffer search overhead /
complexity would be worse with per-queue multiple bufpools, but with
the free lists you mentioned, I think you could do the same thing here
with a free list at the fc level for bufpools across all queues, and
that would address the search concern.

Thanks,
Joanne

With the separation between non-IO queues and IO-queues, I think we
could just look at the request type, and dispatch accordingly so that
the metadata ops go to the non-io queues and the read/write ops go to
the io queues. I think the dispatch logic would be otherwise the same
for choosing which particular queue within the non-io or io queues.

> start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
> scale up based on need? Let's say we have:
>
> Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
> allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
> now additionally we get for the same CPU
>
> - multiple queues of the same buf size
> - multiple queues of different buf sizes
>

I'm not seeing why this would be an issue. For multiple queues of the
same buffer size, it would just be normal queue selection and for
different buffer sizes, we'd jjust have two pools of queues (non-io
and io) and select based on request type first which pool then apply
normal queue selection within that pool. I think if a server has needs
for multiple categories of buffer sizes beyond just non-io vs io,
having multiple bufpools per queue makes more sense than dedicated
queues to the specific categories of the different buffer sizes, but I
doubt this level of granularity would be needed.

>
> Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
> anymore.

Syscall aggregation still applies within each ring, but I don't think
sysclal aggregation is really a big factor given that libfuse and the
server handles requests as they arrive and respond as soon as the
request is handled, instead of batching multiple responses before
submitting; on the dispatch side, requests are sent one by one as they
come in rather than batched, so there's no aggregation to lose.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.summary: The author addresses Miklos Szeredi's concern about partitioning memory into buffers up front and simplifying the interface by having the kernel manage the buffer pool. The author agrees that the buffer size is redundant and could be removed, but notes that adding bufsize to the uapi would make it forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools per queue in the future.

> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
keep it as is then?

>
> > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > comes up in the future,
>
> Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> initialization is a good idea regardless.

I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
happy to make this change.

>
> > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
>
> Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
>
>  - fuse's waiting list
>  - cqe's on the ring
>
> Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
I'm not seeing.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring for the fuse file system. It allows servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool, eliminating the overhead of pinning and unpinning user pages per I/O request. The server must register the payload buffers through io-uring and set specific flags during registration. This patch also includes kernel support for registered buffers advertised via a single umbrella flag. Benchmarks show a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads, 4% for direct sequential reads, 8% for buffered randreads, and 6% for buffered sequential reads.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi questioned the addition of a new parameter init_flags to the fuse_uring_bufpool_setup function without providing a specific reason or context.
review_comments.raw_body:
Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
wondering...

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about pinned pages being accounted for and limited by the kernel io-uring infrastructure, confirming that this is indeed handled.

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern that registered buffers should be supported without the buffer pool feature, explaining that it would add complexity and reduce test surface area. Author believes servers wanting registered buffers also want buffer pools, so no wanted use case is being left out.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

---

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy operations over io-uring in the fuse file system. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch introduces a new feature that requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities, which enables the server to register relevant pages into its io-uring registered buffer table. This allows for more efficient read/write operations by reducing memory copies. The patch also includes documentation of the request flow for zero-copy write path.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: reviewer questioned how FIXED_READ works in userspace and asked for clarification on its interaction with zero-copy io-uring
sentiment_signals: lack of clear technical concern or suggestion
review_comments.raw_body:
FIXED_READ takes an address, right?  How does all this work at the
userspace level?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the FIXED_READ call in userspace, explaining that it takes a buf_index instead of an address and clarifying how the zero_copy_index is used to register pages for zero-copy operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.
Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that
sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/io_uring_prep_read_fixed.3.html

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the fixed operations in io-uring require both a buffer index and an address to determine the offset within the registered buffer, which is not currently provided.

Reviewer noted that passing a NULL address to the fixed op in io-uring operations for zero-copy buffers results in an offset of zero, and questioned whether this is correct behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
AFAICS buf_index is required, but is not sufficient.  The fixed ops
require an address as well (to figure out the offset within the
registered buffer).

---

Okay so there are two buffers per sqe, one which is corresponds to a
real userspace address, and a zero copy one that is (by definition)
kernel-only and doesn't actually have an address.

Passing a NULL address to the fixed op seems okay in this case, but
that means the offset within the buffer is always zero.

Or am I misunderstanding something?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the calculation of the offset into the fixed buffer for zero-copied registered buffers, explaining that io-uring internally sets the buffer's uaddr as 0 and the liburing API computes the offset by subtracting imu->ubuf from the provided address. The author did not indicate any plans to change their code.
review_comments.raw_body:
For zero-copied registered buffers, the "address" you pass in is the
raw offset into the buffer (eg if you want to read into the
zero-copied buffer starting at offset 4k in the buffer, you would call
io_uring_prep_read_fixed(sqe, fd, (void *)4096, len, file_off,
buf_index);).

On the kernel side, fuse uring calls io_buffer_register_bvec() to
place the application pages or page cache pages into the slot for the
registered buffer [1]. io-uring internally sets that buffer's uaddr as
0 (imu->ubuf = 0 in io_kernel_buffer_init() [2]) since the pages are
supplied kernel-side. The liburing io_uring_prep_read_fixed() api will
call into the kernel io_read_fixed() -> io_init_rw_fixed() ->
io_import_reg_buf() -> io_import_fixed(). In io_import_fixed(), the
offset into the fixed buffer is computed with "offset = buf_addr -
imu->ubuf" [3] where buf_addr is the u64 representation of the "void
*addr" that was passed into io_uring_prep_read_fixed().

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-4-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-3-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[3] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/io_uring/rsrc.c#L1072

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi requested a variant of the _fixed API for io-uring that takes an offset instead of an address, which would simplify handling for plain registered buffers and eliminate the need to look up addresses in cqe.
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay.  Could we have a variant of the _fixed API that takes an offset
instead of an address?

That would work much better for the plain registered buffers too, as
the fuse server wouldn't have to look up the address belonging to a
particular buf_index, just supply the offset that was returned in cqe
(as you suggested) to the _fixed op.

Hmm?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that adding a variant of the _fixed API for zero-copy operations is a good idea, and plans to implement it

The author addressed a concern about the difference in address handling between zero-copied and non-zero-copied registered buffers, explaining that for non-zero-copied buffers, the fuse server would use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and pass in the starting address plus offset.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is a liburing API [1]. We could add a variant of the _fixed API
for this (will need to come up with a good name for it...). Internally
it would still have to set sqe->addr = offset, as in [2], but I think
this is a good idea to make it less confusing.

---

For plain (non-zero-copied) registered buffers, the fuse server would
still need to pass in the starting address of the buffer pool, so I
think it would just use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and
pass in starting_address + offset. It's only for buffers that are
zero-copied (eg pages supplied by the kernel) that the addr is the
offset; for non-zero-copied buffers, the addr must be an actual uaddr.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://github.com/axboe/liburing/blob/650d8fb7acb5952a12530b617f09da75b95c979c/src/include/liburing.h#L673
[2] https://github.com/axboe/liburing/blob/650d8fb7acb5952a12530b617f09da75b95c979c/src/include/liburing.h#L604

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested adding a flag to the _FIXED io-uring op to indicate that the offset is being supplied in place of the address, allowing unconditional passing of the offset
review_comments.raw_body:
Why?  The kernel couldn't care less about the userspace address at
that point, it's literally just using the stored and supplied uaddr's
to calculate the offset into the buffer.

Adding a flag to the _FIXED op to indicate that the offset is being
supplied in place of the address should allow unconditionally passing
the offset to these ops.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that zero-copy over io-uring requires additional support in the kernel uapi, agreeing it's not currently implemented as described.

Author addressed a concern about liburing API consistency by explaining that the existing kernel IORING_OP_READ_FIXED op already interprets 'addr' as an offset for pages, and believes it would be consistent to apply this logic to plain registered buffers too.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, but I don't think this is how it's currently supported on the
io-uring side in the kernel. There would need to be added support for
this in the kernel uapi.

---

Oh, I thought the issue from your first message about the liburing api
was that offset gets passed in via the "address" arg? For the existing
kernel IORING_OP_READ_FIXED op, "addr" is already interpreted as an
"offset" for pages that were kernel-supplied into the registered
buffer, so I don't think it'd be inconsistent to also allow plain
registered buffers to do that too.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how buffer pools decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage and enabling optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. It also describes how to use this feature by setting a flag during registration and providing the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first submission queue element (sqe). The documentation includes a high-level overview of how fuse manages the buffer pool internally, including how buffers are selected, copied, and recycled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. The decoupling of ents and buffers also eliminates the need for dedicated payload buffers, reducing memory waste. Additionally, the series introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author simplified the logic for fetching and sending off the next request, removing duplicated code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), and decoupling request fetching from the send operation.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring, explaining that they have decoupled them by allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool (a contiguous allocation of buffers) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. Buffer pool usage is set on a per-ring basis, and the kernel manages buffer selection and recycling through a buffer ring data structure.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of using registered buffers by explaining that they require setting an init flag and following standard protocol for using registered buffers, which is advertised to userspace via the FUSE_HAS_IO_URING_BUFPOOL init flag. The author provided benchmark results showing around a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and a 35% improvement for zero-copy.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of the zero-copy implementation, explaining that it is necessary to handle both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments in a single request flow. The author provided detailed diagrams of the request flow for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered using io_buffer_register_bvec() and io_buffer_unregister(). The author also clarified that some requests may have both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments, which are handled separately.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's feedback about the buffer pool interface being inconsistent and suggested a new command op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) to pass in queue-level settings, including the number of reduced queues and bufpool. The author also explained that returning the buffer address directly would require 8 bytes, but using buf_id takes up only 2 bytes and allows for easier incremental buffer consumption in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:06 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > Allow servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool.
> > When the server registers buffers with io-uring, the pages backing the
> > buffers are pinned upfront. This eliminates the overhead of
> > pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o
> > request. This also allows servers to use the same registered buffers for
> > subsequent backing store I/O (eg read_fixed/write_fixed), keeping data
> > in the same pinned pages without additional pinning or mapping overhead
> > required.
>
> Are pinned pages accounted and limited?

Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

>
> > Registered buffers require the buffer pool (FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL) to be
> > enabled.
>
> Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
> wondering...

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > payload in the first sqe.
>
> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

>
> Thanks,
> Miklos

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:33 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > Buffer ring request flow
> > ------------------------
> > |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> > |                                          |
> > |  [client request arrives]                |
> > |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> > |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> > |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> > |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> > |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> > |                                          |  [CQE received]
> > |                                          |  [read headers]
> > |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
>
> How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

>
> Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

>
> We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
> patchset).  What about a new directory:
> Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

> >                  ent->zero_copy_index]      |
> > |        [ent->zero_copied = true]          |
> > |                                           |
> > |      >fuse_copy_args()                    |

> > |         for page arg, skip data copy]     |
> > |                                           |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring(RING_ENT)         |
> > |      [memcpy ent_in_out]                  |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                   |
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [CQE received]
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [issue io_uring READ at
> > |                                           |  ent->zero_copy_index]
>
> FIXED_READ takes an address, right?  How does all this work at the
> userspace level?

The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.
Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that
sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/io_uring_prep_read_fixed.3.html

With the separation between non-IO queues and IO-queues, I think we
could just look at the request type, and dispatch accordingly so that
the metadata ops go to the non-io queues and the read/write ops go to
the io queues. I think the dispatch logic would be otherwise the same
for choosing which particular queue within the non-io or io queues.

> start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
> scale up based on need? Let's say we have:
>
> Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
> allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
> now additionally we get for the same CPU
>
> - multiple queues of the same buf size
> - multiple queues of different buf sizes
>

I'm not seeing why this would be an issue. For multiple queues of the
same buffer size, it would just be normal queue selection and for
different buffer sizes, we'd jjust have two pools of queues (non-io
and io) and select based on request type first which pool then apply
normal queue selection within that pool. I think if a server has needs
for multiple categories of buffer sizes beyond just non-io vs io,
having multiple bufpools per queue makes more sense than dedicated
queues to the specific categories of the different buffer sizes, but I
doubt this level of granularity would be needed.

>
> Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
> anymore.

Syscall aggregation still applies within each ring, but I don't think
sysclal aggregation is really a big factor given that libfuse and the
server handles requests as they arrive and respond as soon as the
request is handled, instead of batching multiple responses before
submitting; on the dispatch side, requests are sent one by one as they
come in rather than batched, so there's no aggregation to lose.

Thanks,
Joanne

> > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> > memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> > large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> > times.
>
> Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
>
> We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
keep it as is then?

>
> > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > comes up in the future,
>
> Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> initialization is a good idea regardless.

I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
happy to make this change.

>
> > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
>
> Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
>
>  - fuse's waiting list
>  - cqe's on the ring
>
> Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
I'm not seeing.

Thanks,
Joanne

> > > more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed

> > > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the

Ignore the last two sentences please, I didn't think through your
original message.

I like your idea a lot about just passing a chunk of memory to the
kernel and offloading the management logic entirely to the kernel.
Maybe I'm missing something obvious, but why would multiple pools per
queue still be needed? If the server needs more capacity, why not just
have the server spin up a new queue? That would have the included
benefit of adding more concurrency, and dynamically adding more queues
is a feature that would be added to fuse uring regardless. For
multipools in one queue, the pools would be discontiguous anyways,
which wouldn't help with fragmentation. I don't think adding a queue
incurs that much memory or cpu overhead, and imo the scenarios where a
server would want to just add more capacity but not parallelism are
uncommon.

>

> don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
> QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
> happy to make this change.
>
> >
> > > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
> >

> > going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> > types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.
>
> Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
> metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
> requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
> metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
> requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

Thinking about this some more, I think the kernel could just
pre-reserve some ring entries specifically for metadata ops / non-IO
requests. That solves it on the kernel side but the userspace side
would still need a threadpool of some sort if its backend IO blocks
its queue thread.

Thanks,
Joanne

>
> This series is on top of commit f21c8b1a3b740 in for-next.
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see

review_comments.summary: Reviewer Bernd Schubert expressed concerns that a fixed number of queues and queue entries is not suitable for most use cases, and suggested using a config setting to prepare the kernel for dynamic routing updates.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/16/26 07:28, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41\u202fAM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>>
>> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>>> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
>>> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
>>> payload in the first sqe.
>>
>> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
>> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
> 
> Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
> nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
> right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
> registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
> two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
> completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
> like a reasonable tradeoff.
> 
> Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Can we please discuss this on Thursday? I'm by no means opposed to a
config SQE, but I don't see how it will really help. My issue is that
any fixed number of queues and queue entries is almost never the right one.
The config setting (via SQE, ioctl or FUSE_INIT) could be used to
prepare the kernel, but final routing update needs to be dynamic.


Thanks,
Bernd

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds support for io-uring buffer pools to the fuse file system, allowing userspace to pass a chunk of memory to the kernel and offloading management logic. The approach taken is to implement buffer rings, which can be used for both metadata operations and backend IO requests. However, the need for multiple pools per queue is questioned, with the suggestion that dynamically adding new queues could provide more concurrency and capacity as needed.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the queue selection mechanism, noting that it's unclear whether queues are assigned per CPU or per CPU and buffer, which could lead to a 2D matrix and non-optimal routing.

Reviewer pointed out that the proposed solution of having multiple pools per queue is similar to the fuse server specifying ring entries with small buffers for metadata operations, and questioned the necessity of this approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
How do you select the queue - is the queue now per CPU or is the queue
2D per CPU and per buffers? If per CPU, what is if you already have
queues on all CPUs and still want to add buffers?
If per CPU and buf, gets a 2D matrix - gets difficult to find a suitable
queue.
And even if queues are per CPU and enough free CPUs available - might
cause some non-optimal routing.

Maybe I miss some simple logic.

---

Isn't that the same as fuse server saying "here are ring entries with
small buffers for meta ops or other small IO"?


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author addressed reviewer's concern about supporting multiple buffer pools per queue by explaining that having separate queues for each pool is sufficient and dynamically adding new queues would be beneficial, implying a fix is planned in the future.

Author acknowledged that the issue of metadata requests being backed up waiting for IO requests to be finished is a problem, but proposed a kernel-side solution by pre-reserving ring entries specifically for metadata ops / non-IO requests.
review_comments.raw_body:
Ignore the last two sentences please, I didn't think through your
original message.

I like your idea a lot about just passing a chunk of memory to the
kernel and offloading the management logic entirely to the kernel.
Maybe I'm missing something obvious, but why would multiple pools per
queue still be needed? If the server needs more capacity, why not just
have the server spin up a new queue? That would have the included
benefit of adding more concurrency, and dynamically adding more queues
is a feature that would be added to fuse uring regardless. For
multipools in one queue, the pools would be discontiguous anyways,
which wouldn't help with fragmentation. I don't think adding a queue
incurs that much memory or cpu overhead, and imo the scenarios where a

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned the need for partitioning memory into buffers upfront and suggested simplifying the interface by removing redundant buffer size information.

Reviewer suggested separating buffer registration into a separate operation as an initial step towards supporting multiple bufpools, and noted that this does not require immediate support for multiple bufpools but is beneficial for future-proofing

Reviewer noted that 'queue' is ambiguous in the context of fuse and io-uring, and suggested clarifying its meaning to avoid confusion.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?

We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

---

Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
initialization is a good idea regardless.

---

Note, "queue" could mean two things here:

 - fuse's waiting list
 - cqe's on the ring

Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that returning a buffer offset instead of ID would be cleaner, but questions whether adding bufsize to the uapi is necessary for future-proofing.

Author initially disagreed with splitting the buffer pool setup into two functions, but agreed to make the change if it's deemed necessary.

Author is addressing a concern about the potential for IO requests to be backed up by metadata operations, even if separate waiting queues and kernel prioritization are implemented. She asks if she's missing something and also questions the feasibility of implementing threadpools on the userspace side.
sentiment_signals: questioned the necessity of an additional change
review_comments.raw_body:
That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
keep it as is then?

---

I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
happy to make this change.

---

Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
I'm not seeing.

Thanks,
Joanne
