patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to allow high-priority non-blocking allocations to use existing MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserves on the buddy fastpath, rather than falling back to other migratetypes. The ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC flag is updated to only provide access to these reserves without permission to grow them, and a new flag ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC_RESERVE is introduced to specifically allow growing the reserves. This change improves performance in certain workloads by reducing the number of highatomic misses.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested replacing the new alloc flag ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC_RESERVE with a check for ALLOC_WMARK_MIN to determine when to reserve high-atomic pageblocks, eliminating the need for another flag.

The reviewer noted that the patch introduces a mismatch between the fastpath and slowpath allocation flags, specifically allowing highatomic reserves to be tapped for costly orders in the slowpath but not in the fastpath.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch introduces duplicated logic in gfp_to_alloc_flags(), suggesting a more robust approach by providing an alternative implementation

The reviewer suggested optimizing the code by reusing the existing function gfp_to_alloc_flags_nonblocking() instead of calling gfp_to_alloc_flags() and then applying a bitwise AND operation.
review_comments.raw_body:
You could check ALLOC_WMARK_MIN to determine the slowpath. This way
you wouldn't need another alloc flag:

	/* Slowpath (precarious) high-atomic allocation. Maybe reserve block */
	if (unlikely((alloc_flags & (ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC|ALLOC_WMARK_MIN)) == (ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC|ALLOC_WMARK_MIN)))
		reserve_highatomic_pageblock(page, order, zone);

[ we really ought to generalize gfp_has_flags() ... ]

---

There seems to be a mismatch between this and gfp_to_alloc_flags()
(slowpath), where slowpath is still allowed to tap highatomic reserves
for costly orders. Is that on purpose?

---

This duplicates gfp_to_alloc_flags() logic which seems fragile. How
about the below:

---

alloc_flags |= gfp_to_alloc_flags(gfp, order) & ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC;

Or factor gfp_to_alloc_flags_nonblocking() from gfp_to_alloc_flags()
and reuse that here, to save a few cycles in the fast path.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

[PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for delivering NMI-like events to interrupt-masked arm64 CPUs using the SDEI software-signalled event mechanism. The goal is to provide a more efficient way to deliver such events, avoiding the pseudo-NMI hot-path cost. The first user of this feature is arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(), which can now reach masked CPUs without falling back to an IPI path when SDEI is available. A stub implementation is provided for cases where SDEI is not present or disabled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the arm_sdei firmware driver, which signals a software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function is designed to be safe to call from non-maskable interrupt (NMI) or crash context and takes no locks. It uses the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call to deliver the event NMI-like even when interrupts are masked on the target PE. The patch also updates the include files to reflect the new function.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). This is necessary for certain debug and observability features, such as capturing CPU backtraces and crash dumps, which require interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI to deliver the NMI at a lower cost. This allows for more accurate debugging and observability features without introducing significant performance regressions.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch series updates the x86/mm code to use READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE for accessing mm->context.untag_mask, a single aligned word that is written once and never reverted. The changes are made to make lockless access explicit and prevent compiler reloads or tearing.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] mm/pagewalk: let folio_walk_start() run under the per-VMA lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the folio_walk_start() function to run under the per-VMA lock instead of the mmap lock, reducing contention and improving performance for callers that only need to read a single page. The per-VMA lock is used when the FW_VMA_LOCKED flag is passed, which requires RCU-freed page tables and excludes hugetlb VMAs. This change does not affect existing callers, as none currently pass the FW_VMA_LOCKED flag. The patch also adds documentation to explain the new flag and its requirements.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] mm: read remote memory without the mmap lock where possible
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an opportunistic, read-only fast path to the Linux kernel's memory access functions that allows reading remote memory without taking the mmap lock when possible. The existing slow path still uses the mmap lock for reads and writes. The new fast path checks VMA permissions and grabs a short-lived reference to a present page before copying it out. If the page is not present, or if there are other issues like hugepages or I/O mappings, it falls back to the slow path. This change aims to reduce contention on large machines by avoiding unnecessary locking for common read operations.

review_comments.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that using per-VMA locks in access_remote_vm_fast() could lead to inconsistent data when the read spans multiple VMAs, as neighboring VMAs can be unmapped and remapped while reading, and suggested checking if the entire read is contained within one VMA before falling back to mmap_lock.

Reviewer noted that the patchset could be simplified if CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK is removed, referencing a separate patchset by Dave Hansen that achieves this.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think we should be using per-VMA locks if the read spans
multiple VMAs. Doing that would risk a possibility of reading
inconsistent data since we are locking one VMA at a time. While we
load and read VMA, its neighboring VMA can be unmapped and another one
can be mapped in its place. So, our read spanning both VMAs will
return inconsistent data. access_remote_vm_fast() can check if the
entire read is contained within one VMA and if not, fall back to
mmap_lock.

---

Just FYI, Dave Hansen is working on a patchset [1] that removes
CONFIG_PER_VMA_LOCK and makes per-VMA locks always available. This
would simplify this patchset too.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/all/20260610230409.A44D29FA@davehans-spike.ostc.intel.com/

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Usama Arif noted that the patch should also perform FOLL_ANON check from check_vma_flags() to reject non-anonymous VMAs when FOLL_ANON is set, referencing a specific commit and CVE-2018-1120

Reviewer noted that the new resident-page fast path does not call folio_mark_accessed(), which changes reclaim behavior for pages repeatedly read through access_remote_vm().
review_comments.raw_body:
This should also do the FOLL_ANON check from check_vma_flags().

check_vma_flags() rejects non-anonymous VMAs when FOLL_ANON is set:

	if ((gup_flags & FOLL_ANON) && !vma_anon)
		return -EFAULT;

That flag is used by fs/proc/base.c for /proc/PID/cmdline and
/proc/PID/environ.  It was added by commit 7f7ccc2ccc2e ("proc: do not
access cmdline nor environ from file-backed areas"), which fixed
CVE-2018-1120 by making those proc files refuse file-backed argv/env
areas.

---

__access_remote_vm() slow path calls get_user_page_vma_remote() which calls
get_user_pages_remote(). get_user_pages_remote() adds FOLL_TOUCH and then the
page-table walk eventually reaches follow_page_pte().

The new resident-page fast path copies the same data without doing an
equivalent folio_mark_accessed().

That changes reclaim behaviour for pages repeatedly read through
access_remote_vm(), such as /proc/PID/cmdline polling. I think you
should mark the folio as accessed.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a fast path for __access_remote_vm that uses per-VMA locks instead of the process-wide mmap_lock, improving system monitoring reliability and speeding up __access_remote_vm under contention. This is made possible by the introduction of per-VMA locks in the kernel and is only enabled when it's safe to do so. The patch series includes code changes and benchmarking, but still requires further cleanup.

review_comments.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Suren Baghdasaryan asked about the implications of CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE being 'semi RCU safe' on the patch's approach, noting that this mechanism may pose an issue and requesting further clarification.
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks for the patchset Rik!
Previously when I looked into using per-VMA locks in
access_remote_vm(), the biggest hurdle was get_user_pages_remote(),
which required mmap_lock. Your implementation avoids altogether and
keeps the code much simpler than what I expected. I very much support
this approach and will start reviewing in more details.
One question: CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE still has this comment
about "Semi RCU freeing of the page directories." [1]. Does that
mechanism being "semi RCU safe" pose an issue here? I'll need to
refresh my memory on why it's only semi-RCU safe but you might have
the answer ready.

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/mm/mmu_gather.c#L236

Thanks,
Suren.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested that the patch should also handle per-VMA locks for GUP (get_user_pages) operations, which would improve reliability and performance
review_comments.raw_body:
But, wouldn't we, in general, also want to teach GUP to just work with per-VMA
locks?

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Suren Baghdasaryan
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch does not address the lock ordering issue in vswap_free() when calling try_to_unmap(), and requested a fix

Reviewer noted that using gup_fast in access_remote_vm is problematic because it assumes operation on current->mm, not the remote one, and suggested that reworking GUP to work with per-VMA locks would be even more challenging
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 2:42 AM David Hildenbrand (Arm)
<david@kernel.org> wrote:

---

Matthew suggested using gup_fast in access_remote_vm before, and I
looked into that. The biggest issue there is that gup_fast assumes it
always operates on current->mm, not on the remote one. Reworking that
is quite an undertaking.
Teaching GUP in general to work with per-VMA locks I think would also
be much harder than what this patchset does and would require a GUP
expert (which I am unfortunately not).

get_user_pages_remote(). get_user_pages_remote() adds FOLL_TOUCH and then the

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
reviewer noted that the patch may have issues with synchronization between CPUs due to lack of IPI sent for remote MM, but their memory is fuzzy on this point

Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the current implementation of __access_remote_vm still requires walking the complete page table for each individual PTE, which is suboptimal and can be improved by teaching ordinary GUP to run under the VMA lock or creating a new GUP interface that consumes a VMA instead of an MM.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right, that's more tricky, IIRC the CPU from a remote MM might not get an IPI
sent to sync. (but my memory is fuzzy on that)

---

Well, "harder" is not really an excuse ;)

Where a folio_walk really shines at is that you can just walk a PMD entry and
process it all at once, instead of returning 512. Where it doesn't shine is that
you have to walk the complete page table again for each individual PTE.

... which is also what we do right now through get_user_page_vma_remote(), which
is rather suboptimal.

Ideally, you'd obtain multiple page ranges (with upper limit on the ranges) in
one shot, whereby each page range belongs to the same compound page, and there
is exactly one page/folio ref on a range. [we discussed that in other context
recently]

Then, you can just let GUP do the GUP work, without re-implementing it for some
special cases elsewhere. And others can benefit from the work.


So I'd really like us to find out what it would take to teach ordinary GUP (or
better, some new GUP interface) to run under the VMA lock. We can start with the
existing interface to GUP a single page to KIS.

Maybe having a new GUP interface that consumes a VMA instead of an MM could be
the first start to enable per-VMA locks?

All GUP does is walk page tables and call fault handlers. userfaultfd is nasty,
but existing page faults must also deal with that having to fallback to the MM
lock, so it sounds like a solvable problem with some churn?

-- 
Cheers,

David

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v3 3/3] iova: defer maple tree erase on GFP_ATOMIC failure
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: Proposal to add a special entry in the maple tree to represent gaps, allowing for more efficient gap search and re-use.

review_comments.author: Rik van Riel
review_comments.summary: Raised several technical concerns about introducing a special entry for gaps, including gap calculation code changes, edge case handling, and data consistency during rebalancing.
